title: Why Human Passwords Break So Easily

lesson.learn:
Passwords are weak because people prefer secrets that are memorable, and memorable secrets are often predictable. Attackers also benefit from reuse: if one password leaks, the same password may unlock other accounts. That is why length, uniqueness, and password managers matter so much.

A strong password is less about cleverness and more about randomness. The more it looks like a pattern a human would choose, the easier it is to guess or crack.
lesson.simpler: Passwords fail when people make them easy to remember. Strong ones are long, unique, and hard for attackers to predict.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Password strength comes from randomness, length, and uniqueness, not from being easy to remember.
lesson.deeperDive:
Attackers use brute force, phishing, credential stuffing, and leaked databases to break weak logins. Once passwords are stored, good systems hash and salt them so the original secret is not kept in plain form. But even strong hashing cannot rescue a password that has already been reused somewhere else.

That is why modern security advice keeps moving toward password managers, multifactor authentication, and passkeys. The goal is to stop depending on human memory as the main defense.
lesson.funFact: A reused password can fail dozens of accounts at once.
references: Have I Been Pwned - breach awareness resources

people: Bill Burr - helped popularize older password rules
Kevin Mitnick - famous social engineering case study
Whitfield Diffie - helped shape modern cryptography
Moxie Marlinspike - advanced secure messaging design
places: Login screens - where password policy matters
Password manager apps - secure storage for secrets
Breach databases - places attackers look for reuse
Security operations centers - monitoring for credential attacks
years: 2020s - passkeys and MFA become more important
concepts: Entropy - how unpredictable a password is
Hashing - turning a password into a protected value
Salting - adding unique data before hashing
Credential stuffing - trying leaked passwords everywhere
Multifactor authentication - using more than one proof of identity
